narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 45.4% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is high enough to raise concerns about your long-term success. Looking at your profile, you ranked in the 2nd decile for grades in both your first and third years of school—much lower than the typical student's 6th decile or so—while your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 falls below the average of 3.21, and your LSAT score of 36 is slightly under the usual 36.7. Your family falls into the middle income bracket, you're male, white, and planning full-time studies, all of which align closely with the majority of applicants in our dataset. When the model made its decision, your third-year decile rank had the strongest influence, pushing the prediction toward failure because it was so much lower than what the model sees in students who typically pass. Next in importance was your first-year decile rank, which similarly pulled the odds downward compared to stronger performers. Your LSAT score came third, as being a bit below average nudged the prediction unfavorably, followed by your middle family income, undergraduate GPA, racial background, gender, and full-time status, each contributing a bit more toward the failure risk in that order. Overall, these factors together painted a picture riskier than the typical applicant, who tends to have higher early rankings and GPAs that signal better bar exam readiness. While demographics like gender, race, and family income are harder to change, you can focus on boosting your academic standing—perhaps by excelling in any remaining coursework or law school prep to demonstrate growth beyond those early deciles. Retaking the LSAT for a higher score could also help in future applications, as even small improvements there carry weight. In the meantime, targeted bar exam prep courses have helped many students in similar positions overcome early academic hurdles and pass on the first try. We believe in your potential, and refining these key areas could flip the script for your next opportunity. Hang in there—you've got this with the right steps forward.
